title: 1.1: Explains how directory walking collects files with pattern-based filtering (e.g., walk function using filepath.WalkDir, allowed function checking glob patterns like *~ and #*#)
1.2: Identifies the Collection struct role in tracking comparison categories (e.g., changes map, renames map, adds set, removes set, type_map)
1.3: Explains how content hashing detects renamed files (e.g., MD5 hash comparison via hash_for_path function, matching hashes indicating same content)
1.4: Explains the anchored diff algorithm's O(n log n) complexity (e.g., unique lines as anchors, tgs function using binary search, Thomas G. Szymanski approach)
1.5: Explains how unique lines are identified (e.g., counting occurrences with -1 for old file, -4 for new file, total -5 marking unique lines)
1.6: Explains the LRU caching system (e.g., 4096 entry capacity, seven caches for size, mimetype, data, is_text, lines, highlighted_lines, hash)
1.7: Explains how syntax highlighting runs in parallel (e.g., highlight_all function, images.Context.Parallel spawning goroutines, worker pattern consuming from channel)
1.8: Explains how text versus binary files are distinguished (e.g., is_path_text function checking MIME type and UTF-8 validity, images detected separately)
1.9: Explains the tgs algorithm producing matching pairs (e.g., sentinel start {0,0}, anchor matches between line indexes, sentinel end {len(x),len(y)})

1.10: Explains how files are matched between left and right directories (e.g., building sets of filenames, set intersection to find common files, files only in left become removals, files only in right become additions)

1.11: Explains how parallel highlighting does not have conflicts (e.g., buffered channel distributing work, sync.WaitGroup coordinating goroutines, each worker processing independent files, sync.RWMutex protecting cache access)
